title: Splunk tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Splunk HTTP Event Collector (HEC) token detected in source code. Splunk HEC tokens are authentication credentials used to send data to Splunk via the HTTP Event Collector API. These tokens follow a GUID/UUID format and provide direct access to ingest data into Splunk indexes. Exposing this token allows attackers to: send unauthorized data to your Splunk instance flooding indexes and consuming license quota, inject malicious or false data to corrupt analytics and security monitoring, send massive volumes of junk data causing denial of service and storage exhaustion, access Splunk deployment information through token metadata, exhaust daily indexing limits disrupting legitimate log collection, manipulate security event data to hide malicious activity or create false alerts, corrupt compliance audit trails and forensic evidence, incur significant costs through excessive data ingestion and storage, cause performance degradation affecting real-time monitoring and alerting, and potentially gain insights into your infrastructure by observing what data formats are accepted. For enterprises with expensive Splunk licenses, token abuse can result in: monthly overage charges (thousands to millions of dollars depending on volume), emergency capacity upgrades, incident response costs for data integrity verification, corrupted security investigations and compliance reports, loss of trust in monitoring and SIEM data, regulatory compliance violations if audit logs are tampered, service degradation affecting SOC operations and threat detection, storage capacity exhaustion requiring infrastructure expansion, and potential contract renegotiation costs. IMMEDIATELY disable this HEC token in Splunk Web (Settings → Data Inputs → HTTP Event Collector → Token Name → Disable), create a new replacement token with restricted permissions (limit to specific indexes and source types), audit all data received using the compromised token checking for anomalies, suspicious sources, or data spikes, review Splunk metrics for unusual ingestion patterns or volume spikes, check index data for malicious injections or corrupted events, verify license usage for unauthorized data volumes, store HEC tokens securely using environment variables (SPLUNK_HEC_TOKEN) never in code, secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager), or encrypted configuration files (never commit to version control, Docker images, logs, or CI/CD pipelines), implement least privilege by creating separate HEC tokens for different data sources with index-level restrictions and source type filtering, enable token allowlists restricting tokens to specific source IP addresses or CIDR ranges, use SSL/TLS certificates for mutual authentication when possible, set up monitoring and alerting for: unusual data volume from specific tokens, new source types or sources not previously seen, data ingestion from unexpected IP addresses or geographic locations, tokens approaching or exceeding quota limits, and failed authentication attempts, rotate HEC tokens regularly (every 90 days recommended), maintain audit logs of token creation and modification, use Splunk App for Infrastructure or monitoring console to track token usage patterns, implement data validation and sanitization at ingestion points, set up index quotas and retention policies to limit blast radius, enable input validation rules to reject malformed or suspicious data, configure Splunk forwarder authentication where applicable instead of direct HEC where possible, and deploy Splunk intrusion detection to monitor for data injection attacks or anomalies in real-time.
